Diamond Grape, 31 May 2026Poco software are really bloated while T series aren't. I've used both a poco x3 pro... morePoco X3 Pro is a budget phone. 
Plus all ads can get disabled, it takes 2 minutes to do it from the app menu and you never have to tweak anything else ever.

The 17T is literally a Poco X8 Pro with the only reason to exist for literally double the price being better cameras. Everything else is exactly the same (optical reader fo 750€? Really?) or worse (15 minute charge rate is 5:21 which is bordering on Sony / Pixel territory and slower than the much memed iPhones, though in Europe where you don't get the special sauce charger in the box they'll probably perform similar) than that phone. It's also in the 'liked by nobody' size where it's too big to be a small (or as close as 6.3 inches gets to 'small') phone and too small to be a big phone at that price point. It's one of those phones that feel like they're made for nobody. For that price difference you could get the X8 Pro (even the Pro Max) and a better camera for what you're saving if you care. 
 
The 17T Pro is somehow even more confusing. The screen there's nothing wrong with, the battery life is very good, charging rate is very strong (9:06 from 15 minutes is a very strong result)... but all of that's also true of the OnePlus 15, which is around the same price, charges even faster (over 10 hours from 15 minutes) and has an ultrasonic fingerprint reader so it might actually work. Only thing going for this is the slightly better main camera. 
 
Overall they both... just exist at this price, the Pro is better but I can't recommend either of them.

Anonymous, 31 May 2026Seems like they backed off iirc. And verification was replaced with waiting for 24 hours to in... moreFrom KeepAndroidOpen: 
 
Google says "power users" can "still install" unverified apps. Here's what that actually looks like: 
1. Delve into System Settings, find Developer Options 
2. Tap the build number seven times to enable Developer Mode 
3. Dismiss scare screens about coercion 
4. Enter your PIN 
5. Restart the device 
6. Wait 24 hours 
7. Come back, dismiss more scare screens 
8. Pick "allow temporarily" (7 days) or "allow indefinitely" 
9. Confirm, again, that you understand "the risks" 
 
Nine steps. A mandatory 24-hour cooling-off period. For installing software on a device you own. 
 
Worse: this flow runs entirely through Google Play Services, not the Android OS. Google can change it, tighten it, or kill it at any time, with no OS update required and no consent needed. And as of today, it hasn't shipped in any beta, preview, or canary build. It exists only as a blog post and some mockups.
